title: ISigmaSeparation
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $A,B,C \ins J \cup V$ (not necessarily disjoint) subset of nodes. We then say that:

  1. $A$ is $i\sigma$-separated from $B$ given $C$ in $G$, in symbols: \[ A \isPerp_G B \given C, \] if every walk from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$ (sic!) is $\sigma$-blocked by $C$.
  2. If that property does not hold we will write: \[ A \nisPerp_G B \given C. \]
  3. We also define the special case: \[ A \isPerp_G B \qquad :\iff \qquad A \isPerp_G B \given \emptyset.\]
  4. For the special case $J = \emptyset$—that is, if $G$ has no input nodes—$i\sigma$-separation is also called $\sigma$-separation, and we write: \[A \sPerp_G B \given C := A \isPerp_G B \given C, \qquad A \nsPerp_G B \given C := A \nisPerp_G B \given C.\]

    Note that $\sigma$-separation is symmetric: $A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C$, since when $J = \emptyset$ the set of walks between $A$ and $B$ is the same regardless of direction, and the $\sigma$-blocking conditions are invariant under walk reversal.

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_18 -- "ISigmaSeparation".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_18
% title: ISigmaSeparation
\def\rowref{def\_3\_18}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_18}

\begin{Def}[$i\sigma$-separation]\label{def:sigma_separation}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}, the walk definition of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i., and the $\sigma$-blocking predicate ``$\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked'' (equivalently ``$\sigma$-blocked given $C$'') of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}. Let $A, B, C \ins J \cup V$ be subsets of nodes; $A$, $B$, $C$ are \emph{not} assumed to be pairwise disjoint, nor disjoint from the input-node set $J$, nor non-empty.

    We then say that:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \emph{$A$ is $i\sigma$-separated from $B$ given $C$ in $G$,} in symbols
            \[
                A \isPerp_G B \given C,
            \]
            iff: for every integer $n \ge 0$ and every walk
            \[
                \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
            \]
            in $G$ (def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.) with $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in J \cup B$\footnote{The choice to include $J$ here in this place is non-standard in the literature. However, if we include $J$ in this definition here the implied (asymmetric) separoid rules for $id$-/$i\sigma$-separation will be of the same form as those for Markov kernels regarding conditional independence. This is the reason we include $J$ here.\label{fn:why-J}}, the walk $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}.

            \emph{Range of the walk quantifier.} The universal quantifier ranges over \emph{all} walks of every admissible length $n \ge 0$, including the trivial (length-zero) walk: whenever there exists $a \in A \cap (J \cup B)$, the tuple $\pi = (a)$ is, by def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ (case $n = 0$), a walk from $a$ to $a$ in $G$ with $v_0 = v_n = a$, hence $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in J \cup B$, and is therefore in the scope of the quantifier. Whether such a trivial walk is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked is determined by applying def \ref{def:sigma_blocking} at $n = 0$ (the blocking status reduces to: is the single end-position $k = 0$ a blockable non-collider with $v_0 \in C$, in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}? --- which is the formalizer's downstream derivation, not part of the literal definition stated here). In particular, the condition is \emph{not} vacuously satisfied when $A \cap (J \cup B)$ is non-empty, and downstream consumers should not assume otherwise.

            \emph{Asymmetric inclusion of $J$ on the right.} The right-hand endpoint constraint $v_n \in J \cup B$ (rather than $v_n \in B$) is the deliberate, non-standard choice flagged in footnote~\ref{fn:why-J} of the LN: with $J$ included on the right, the implied (asymmetric) separoid rules for $i\sigma$-separation match the corresponding rules for conditional independence under Markov kernels. As a direct consequence, the marginal case $B = \emptyset$ is \emph{not} vacuous: $A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$ asserts that every walk in $G$ from a node in $A$ to a node in $J$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked, which is a genuine condition whenever $J \neq \emptyset$. Only when $J = \emptyset$ does $A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$ recover the usual ``separation from the empty set is vacuous'' reading.

        \item \emph{If the property in item~1 does not hold} --- equivalently, if there exist an integer $n \ge 0$ and a walk
            \[
                \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)
            \]
            in $G$ with $v_0 \in A$, $v_n \in J \cup B$, and $\pi$ is \emph{not} $C$-$\sigma$-blocked (equivalently, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}) --- we write
            \[
                A \nisPerp_G B \given C.
            \]

        \item The unconditional ($C = \emptyset$) special case is defined as the abbreviation
            \[
                A \isPerp_G B \quad :\iff \quad A \isPerp_G B \given \emptyset.
            \]
            Unfolded via item~1: $A \isPerp_G B$ iff for every $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi$ in $G$ from a node $v_0 \in A$ to a node $v_n \in J \cup B$, $\pi$ is $\emptyset$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}.

        \item For the special case $J = \emptyset$ --- that is, $G$ has no input nodes --- $i\sigma$-separation is also called \emph{$\sigma$-separation}, and we write
            \[
                A \sPerp_G B \given C \;\; := \;\; A \isPerp_G B \given C,
                \qquad
                A \nsPerp_G B \given C \;\; := \;\; A \nisPerp_G B \given C.
            \]
            Under $J = \emptyset$ the right-hand endpoint constraint $v_n \in J \cup B$ reduces to $v_n \in B$, so $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ unfolds to: for every $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi$ in $G$ from a node $v_0 \in A$ to a node $v_n \in B$, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking}.
    \end{enumerate}

    \emph{Treatment of the trailing LN remark.}
    The LN source block (\texttt{graphs.tex}, \texttt{\textbackslash label\{def:sigma\_separation\}}) closes item~4 with an embedded \texttt{claimmark} stating that $\sigma$-separation is symmetric ($A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C$ when $J = \emptyset$), justified informally by the observation that walks ``between $A$ and $B$'' are the same regardless of direction and that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions are invariant under walk reversal. Per the convention adopted in the sibling row \texttt{def\_3\_17} (whose embedded \texttt{claimmark} for \texttt{claim\_3\_21} is treated identically), that informal pre-statement is \emph{intentionally excluded} from the formalization of def \ref{def:sigma_separation}: it is not part of the definition proper, and its substantive content is the dedicated separate claim row \texttt{claim\_3\_22} (\emph{$\sigma$-separation is symmetric}), whose statement and proof live in \texttt{claim\_3\_22\_statement\_SigmaSeparationSymmetric.tex} and \texttt{claim\_3\_22\_proof\_SigmaSeparationSymmetric.tex} respectively. The row's authoritative \texttt{addition\_to\_the\_LN} clause \texttt{[sigma\_symmetry\_claim\_invokes\_unstated\_reversal\_invariance]} records (as background, not as a Lean obligation within this definition) the precise sense in which the informal justification is to be read: in a CDMG, a walk per def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ is a sequence of incident edges \emph{without regard to traversal orientation}, so reading the tuple in reverse yields another walk with start- and end-nodes swapped; this walk-reversal map is an involution on the set of walks in $G$. Symmetry of $\sigma$-separation (when $J = \emptyset$) is to be understood as resting on this involution together with the fact that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions of def \ref{def:sigma_blocking} on internal positions are stated invariantly under walk reversal --- but this reasoning is the content of the proof of \texttt{claim\_3\_22}, not of the present definition. No Lean obligation is to be derived from the embedded \texttt{claimmark} within this definition.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg, the walk definition of def def:walks, item i., and the $\sigma$-blocking predicate “$\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked” (equivalently “$\sigma$-blocked given $C$”) of def def:sigma_blocking. Let $A, B, C \ins J \cup V$ be subsets of nodes; $A$, $B$, $C$ are not assumed to be pairwise disjoint, nor disjoint from the input-node set $J$, nor non-empty.

We then say that:

  1. $A$ is $i\sigma$-separated from $B$ given $C$ in $G$, in symbols \[ A \isPerp_G B \given C, \] iff: for every integer $n \ge 0$ and every walk \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ (def def:walks, item i.) with $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in J \cup B$, the walk $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def def:sigma_blocking.

    Range of the walk quantifier. The universal quantifier ranges over all walks of every admissible length $n \ge 0$, including the trivial (length-zero) walk: whenever there exists $a \in A \cap (J \cup B)$, the tuple $\pi = (a)$ is, by def def:walks, item i.\ (case $n = 0$), a walk from $a$ to $a$ in $G$ with $v_0 = v_n = a$, hence $v_0 \in A$ and $v_n \in J \cup B$, and is therefore in the scope of the quantifier. Whether such a trivial walk is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked is determined by applying def def:sigma_blocking at $n = 0$ (the blocking status reduces to: is the single end-position $k = 0$ a blockable non-collider with $v_0 \in C$, in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider? — which is the formalizer's downstream derivation, not part of the literal definition stated here). In particular, the condition is not vacuously satisfied when $A \cap (J \cup B)$ is non-empty, and downstream consumers should not assume otherwise.

    Asymmetric inclusion of $J$ on the right. The right-hand endpoint constraint $v_n \in J \cup B$ (rather than $v_n \in B$) is the deliberate, non-standard choice flagged in footnote fn:why-J of the LN: with $J$ included on the right, the implied (asymmetric) separoid rules for $i\sigma$-separation match the corresponding rules for conditional independence under Markov kernels. As a direct consequence, the marginal case $B = \emptyset$ is not vacuous: $A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$ asserts that every walk in $G$ from a node in $A$ to a node in $J$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked, which is a genuine condition whenever $J \neq \emptyset$. Only when $J = \emptyset$ does $A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$ recover the usual “separation from the empty set is vacuous” reading.

  2. If the property in item 1 does not hold — equivalently, if there exist an integer $n \ge 0$ and a walk \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ with $v_0 \in A$, $v_n \in J \cup B$, and $\pi$ is not $C$-$\sigma$-blocked (equivalently, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open in the sense of def def:sigma_blocking) — we write \[ A \nisPerp_G B \given C. \]
  3. The unconditional ($C = \emptyset$) special case is defined as the abbreviation \[ A \isPerp_G B \quad :\iff \quad A \isPerp_G B \given \emptyset. \] Unfolded via item 1: $A \isPerp_G B$ iff for every $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi$ in $G$ from a node $v_0 \in A$ to a node $v_n \in J \cup B$, $\pi$ is $\emptyset$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def def:sigma_blocking.
  4. For the special case $J = \emptyset$ — that is, $G$ has no input nodes — $i\sigma$-separation is also called $\sigma$-separation, and we write \[ A \sPerp_G B \given C \;\; := \;\; A \isPerp_G B \given C, \qquad A \nsPerp_G B \given C \;\; := \;\; A \nisPerp_G B \given C. \] Under $J = \emptyset$ the right-hand endpoint constraint $v_n \in J \cup B$ reduces to $v_n \in B$, so $A \sPerp_G B \given C$ unfolds to: for every $n \ge 0$ and every walk $\pi$ in $G$ from a node $v_0 \in A$ to a node $v_n \in B$, $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-blocked in the sense of def def:sigma_blocking.

Treatment of the trailing LN remark. The LN source block (graphs.tex, \texttt{\label\{def:sigma\_separation\}}) closes item 4 with an embedded claimmark stating that $\sigma$-separation is symmetric ($A \sPerp_G B \given C \iff B \sPerp_G A \given C$ when $J = \emptyset$), justified informally by the observation that walks “between $A$ and $B$” are the same regardless of direction and that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions are invariant under walk reversal. Per the convention adopted in the sibling row def_3_17 (whose embedded claimmark for claim_3_21 is treated identically), that informal pre-statement is intentionally excluded from the formalization of def def:sigma_separation: it is not part of the definition proper, and its substantive content is the dedicated separate claim row claim_3_22 ($\sigma$-separation is symmetric), whose statement and proof live in claim_3_22_statement_SigmaSeparationSymmetric.tex and claim_3_22_proof_SigmaSeparationSymmetric.tex respectively. The row's authoritative addition_to_the_LN clause [sigma_symmetry_claim_invokes_unstated_reversal_invariance] records (as background, not as a Lean obligation within this definition) the precise sense in which the informal justification is to be read: in a CDMG, a walk per def def:walks, item i.\ is a sequence of incident edges without regard to traversal orientation, so reading the tuple in reverse yields another walk with start- and end-nodes swapped; this walk-reversal map is an involution on the set of walks in $G$. Symmetry of $\sigma$-separation (when $J = \emptyset$) is to be understood as resting on this involution together with the fact that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions of def def:sigma_blocking on internal positions are stated invariantly under walk reversal — but this reasoning is the content of the proof of claim_3_22, not of the present definition. No Lean obligation is to be derived from the embedded claimmark within this definition.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `IsISigmaSeparated` and its four notational siblings. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing because the ambient `CDMG Node` structure stores `J, V : Finset Node` and `E, L : Finset (Node × Node)`; decidable equality is what lets those `Finset`s exist and what enables the `(G.J : Set Node)` coercion appearing in the main predicate. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g. `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability backs `CDMG`'s `Finset` fields and the `G.J` coercion below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `IsISigmaSeparated` and its four notational siblings. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing because the ambient `CDMG Node` structure stores `J, V : Finset Node` and `E, L : Finset (Node × Node)`; decidable equality is what lets those `Finset`s exist and what enables the `(G.J : Set Node)` coercion appearing in the main predicate. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g. `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability backs `CDMG`'s `Finset` fields and the `G.J` coercion below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def IsISigmaSeparated (G : CDMG Node) (A B C : Set Node)
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) : Prop :=
  ∀ {u v : Node} (π : Walk G u v),
      u ∈ A → v ∈ (G.J : Set Node) ∪ B → π.IsSigmaBlockedGiven C hC

lean_blocks.explanation: This block introduces `G.IsNotISigmaSeparated A B C` — the LN's `A ⪥ⁱˢ_G B | C` — as a named alias for `¬ G.IsISigmaSeparated A B C`. The same three domain premises `hA`, `hB`, `hC` ride along on the signature so that interchange with the underlying predicate is type-compatible without bridging adapters. The body is a single `¬`, which makes the unfolding `G.IsNotISigmaSeparated A B C hA hB hC = ¬ G.IsISigmaSeparated A B C hA hB hC` hold by `rfl`: any proof that needs to switch between the named negation and the unfolded universal can do so with `unfold IsNotISigmaSeparated` or `simp only [IsNotISigmaSeparated]`, never a classical equivalence lemma. The named-`def` shape is preferred over inlining `¬ IsISigmaSeparated` at every downstream call site because the LN itself introduces `\nisPerp` as named notation; mirroring that with a Lean alias keeps the LN-to-Lean grep one-to-one. The tex's ``equivalently, ∃ walk … not $C$-$\sigma$-blocked'' reformulation is the classical De Morgan dual of the negated universal — a one-line lemma when needed, not a parallel positive-existential definition (which would sever the definitional link with `IsISigmaSeparated`).
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Named negation `A ⪥ⁱˢ_G B | C`; `rfl`-equal to `¬ IsISigmaSeparated`, no classical bridge needed.
def IsNotISigmaSeparated (G : CDMG Node) (A B C : Set Node)
    -- Same three domain premises as the underlying predicate so the signatures stay parallel at call sites.
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) : Prop :=
  -- Body forwards to the negation of the iσ predicate, with all six arguments passed through unchanged.
  ¬ G.IsISigmaSeparated A B C hA hB hC

lean_blocks.code:
abbrev IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty (G : CDMG Node) (A B : Set Node)
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) : Prop :=
  G.IsISigmaSeparated A B ∅ hA hB (Set.empty_subset _)
lean_blocks.explanation: This block introduces the LN's unconditional shorthand `A ⫫ⁱˢ_G B := A ⫫ⁱˢ_G B | ∅` as `G.IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty A B`. The third subset hypothesis is dropped from the call-site signature because it would be the constant `Set.empty_subset _`; supplying it inline keeps the call site to two arguments `(hA hB)`, matching the LN's notation which mentions only `A` and `B`. The use of `abbrev` rather than `def` is load-bearing for ergonomics: `abbrev` is reducibly transparent, so `G.IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty A B hA hB` unfolds to `G.IsISigmaSeparated A B ∅ hA hB (Set.empty_subset _)` at every elaboration site without any explicit `unfold` step. Every lemma about `IsISigmaSeparated` fires automatically on the shorthand and vice versa. A `def` here would create an opaque alias and force `unfold IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty` at every interchange, which the LN's ``is defined as'' reading does not justify. No separate negated counterpart exists at this level — downstream sites needing ``not unconditionally $i\sigma$-separated'' spell it as `¬ G.IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty A B hA hB` or as `G.IsNotISigmaSeparated A B ∅ hA hB (Set.empty_subset _)`, and the `abbrev`'s transparency makes the two interchangeable.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Unconditional shorthand `A ⫫ⁱˢ_G B`; `abbrev` (not `def`) so it reduces to the underlying predicate at every elaboration site.
abbrev IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty (G : CDMG Node) (A B : Set Node)
    -- Two premises only; the third `hC : ∅ ⊆ _` is supplied inline via `Set.empty_subset _` and not exposed at the call site.
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) : Prop :=
  -- Specialise `IsISigmaSeparated` at `C = ∅`; the `Set.empty_subset _` term discharges the missing third hypothesis.
  G.IsISigmaSeparated A B ∅ hA hB (Set.empty_subset _)

lean_blocks.explanation: This block introduces the LN's $\sigma$-separation predicate `G.IsSigmaSeparated hJ A B C` — written `A ⫫_G B | C` in the LN — as the renaming of $i\sigma$-separation for the special case `G.J = ∅`. The premise `hJ : G.J = ∅` enters the signature explicitly: it is the LN's typed-concept distinction between $\sigma$-separation (only available when the input-node set is empty) and $i\sigma$-separation (the general case). The body is a transparent forward to `IsISigmaSeparated`: under `G.J = ∅` the right-endpoint constraint `v ∈ (G.J : Set Node) ∪ B` reduces to `v ∈ B`, but that reduction is a consequence of `hJ` consumed at call sites, not a rewrite baked into the body. The encoding is `def`, not `abbrev`, precisely because `hJ` is a dependent hypothesis: an `abbrev` would unfold eagerly and `hJ` would vanish from goal displays at unrelated tactic steps, erasing the $\sigma$-vs-$i\sigma$ symbolic distinction the LN takes care to maintain. The three domain premises `hA`, `hB`, `hC` are the same as on `IsISigmaSeparated`; the body forwards them unchanged. Downstream consumers such as the $\sigma$-separation symmetry claim, and the chapter-4 Markov-property results, name the predicate via `IsSigmaSeparated` whenever they reason under `G.J = ∅`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `\sigma`-separation `A ⫫_G B | C`; the LN reserves this name for the `G.J = ∅` specialisation of `i\sigma`-separation.
def IsSigmaSeparated (G : CDMG Node) (hJ : G.J = ∅) (A B C : Set Node)
    -- Same `A, B, C ⊆ J ∪ V` domain premises as the underlying iσ predicate; the renaming does not loosen the domain.
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) : Prop :=
  -- Body forwards verbatim to `IsISigmaSeparated`; `hJ` is carried on the signature for LN-faithfulness, not consumed here.
  G.IsISigmaSeparated A B C hA hB hC

lean_blocks.explanation: This block introduces the LN's `A ⪥_G B | C` — ``not $\sigma$-separated'' — as `G.IsNotSigmaSeparated hJ A B C`, the paired negated notation under `G.J = ∅`. The LN introduces `\nsPerp` and `\sPerp` together in item 4, and downstream sites use the two together (claim statements and proof case-splits alternate between $\sigma$-separated and not-$\sigma$-separated), so this declaration mirrors `IsSigmaSeparated`'s signature exactly: the same `(hJ : G.J = ∅)` premise, the same three domain premises `hA`, `hB`, `hC`, the same `def`-not-`abbrev` encoding for the same reason (`hJ` is a dependent hypothesis whose visibility would be lost under `abbrev`-grade reducibility). The body forwards directly to `IsNotISigmaSeparated`; the negated content is not re-derived here. In particular, this declaration does *not* re-introduce a positive-existential reformulation of the negation — the existential dual remains the classical De Morgan lemma noted on `IsNotISigmaSeparated`, shared across both the $i\sigma$ and $\sigma$ names. Together with the preceding four declarations, this completes the LN's four-item separation block as five named, signature-aligned Lean predicates.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `\sigma`-not-separation `A ⪥_G B | C`; the paired negated alias of `IsSigmaSeparated` under `G.J = ∅`.
def IsNotSigmaSeparated (G : CDMG Node) (hJ : G.J = ∅) (A B C : Set Node)
    -- Identical domain premises to the positive `IsSigmaSeparated`; pair is introduced and used as a unit downstream.
    (hA : A ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hB : B ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) (hC : C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V) : Prop :=
  -- Body forwards to the named iσ negation; no new content beyond the σ-rename.
  G.IsNotISigmaSeparated A B C hA hB hC

addition_to_the_LN: [sigma_symmetry_claim_invokes_unstated_reversal_invariance] In a CDMG, a walk is a sequence of incident edges without regard to edge orientation; consequently, every walk from $A$ to $B$ is, read in reverse, a walk from $B$ to $A$, and this reversal is an involution on the set of walks. The symmetry of $\sigma$-separation (when $J = \emptyset$) is to be understood as resting on this walk-reversal involution together with the fact that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions on internal nodes are stated in a manner invariant under reversal.
design_choices:
**`Set Node` (not `Finset Node`) for `A`, `B`, `C`.** The LN's ``$A, B, C \subseteq J \cup V$, not necessarily disjoint, nor disjoint from $J$, nor non-empty'' admits $\emptyset$ and imposes no finiteness obligation. `Set Node` carries the boolean algebra (`∪`, `∩`, `\`, `∅`) needed to spell `J ∪ B` and `C = ∅` directly, interoperates with `G.AncSet` (used inside `IsSigmaBlockedGiven`) without extra coercion, and matches `def_3_17`'s `C : Set Node`. A `Finset Node` shape was considered and rejected: it would force every consumer to supply a `Finset` even in proofs that only need set algebra, would not interoperate with `G.AncSet` directly, and would not reflect the ``not necessarily non-empty'' / arbitrary-subset permissiveness. Consumers holding `s : Finset Node` can always pass `(↑s : Set Node)`.

**Per-set domain premises `hA`, `hB`, `hC` on the predicate, not on downstream theorem sites.** The LN writes ``$A, B, C \subseteq J \cup V$'' once at the head of the separation block; three named per-set premises mirror that exactly and close a silent-admission leak the predicate would otherwise exhibit (a caller could pass arbitrary `Set Node` including nodes that don't exist in `G`, and the predicate would be well-typed under a meaning the LN never assigned it). Pinning the constraint on the def — not on theorem sites — is the only encoding that closes the leak at the source. Separate named hypotheses rather than a bundled `A ∪ B ∪ C ⊆ ↑G.J ∪ ↑G.V` union or an `∧`-conjunction match the chapter-3 convention from `HardInterventionsCommute`, `DisjointHardInterventionsSwig`, and `AddingInterventionNodes`. The bodies do not consume `hA`, `hB`, `hC` — out-of-graph nodes contribute vacuously to the walk universal — so `set_option linter.unusedVariables false in` is sited above each marker block.

**Asymmetric inclusion of `J` on the right endpoint.** LN footnote `fn:why-J` is the load-bearing justification: putting `J` on the right (`v ∈ (G.J : Set Node) ∪ B`) rather than just `B` makes the implied (asymmetric) separoid rules for $id$- / $i\sigma$-separation match those for conditional independence under Markov kernels — load-bearing for chapter 4+ (Markov properties / CBNs). Direct consequence: the marginal case `B = ∅` is *not* vacuous when `G.J ≠ ∅`; `G.IsISigmaSeparated A ∅ C` asserts every walk from `A` into the input nodes `J` is `C`-$\sigma$-blocked. Symmetrising to `J ∪ B ∪ A` on the right or dropping `J` to recover the usual literature convention would break this alignment.

**Endpoint coercion via `(G.J : Set Node) ∪ B`.** `G.J` has type `Finset Node`; `B` has type `Set Node`. The `Finset`-level union is ill-typed (no `Union` instance between `Finset Node` and `Set Node`), so coercing `G.J` to `Set Node` first and unioning at the set level is the only well-typed move. This differs from `def_3_5`'s `NonDesc`, which forms `((G.J ∪ G.V : Finset Node) : Set Node)` because both sides of that inner union are `Finset Node`. The set-union form lifts every `Set`-algebra lemma (`Set.mem_union`, `Set.union_empty`, `Set.empty_union`, `Set.union_comm`) directly to walks of `IsISigmaSeparated`.

**Walk-first universal; length-zero walks in scope.** The walk is the primary subject of `∀ {u v} (π : Walk G u v), …`; endpoint memberships are properties of the walk's endpoints, mirroring `def_3_16`'s `IsBlockableNonCollider` binder shape. The quantifier ranges over both `Walk.nil` ($n = 0$) and `Walk.cons` ($n \ge 1$): when `a ∈ A ∩ (J ∪ B)`, the trivial walk `Walk.nil a _` is admitted. `def_3_17`'s `IsSigmaBlockedGiven` is `False` on `Walk.nil` (its existential over collider / blockable-non-collider positions is empty at length zero), so length-zero walks are $\sigma$-*open*, never $\sigma$-blocked. Practical downstream surprise: `G.IsISigmaSeparated A A C` requires every `a ∈ A`'s trivial self-walk to be `C`-$\sigma$-blocked, which cannot hold for non-empty `A`; consumers reaching for ``separation of `A` from itself = `True`'' should not.

**Named `def` for the negation, definitionally equal to `¬ IsISigmaSeparated`.** LN item 2 introduces `\nisPerp` as named notation for the negation of `\isPerp`, not a new concept. A named `def IsNotISigmaSeparated` keeps every LN reference to `A ⪥ⁱˢ_G B | C` grep-aligned with literal `G.IsNotISigmaSeparated A B C` in Lean. The body `¬ G.IsISigmaSeparated A B C hA hB hC` keeps the two predicates definitionally linked — `unfold IsNotISigmaSeparated` and `simp only [IsNotISigmaSeparated]` collapse it to the negation at any proof site, no separate equivalence lemma. The tex's ``equivalently, $\exists$ walk $\pi$ … not $C$-$\sigma$-blocked'' is the classical De Morgan dual of the negated universal — derivable as a one-line lemma when needed, not a parallel positive-existential definition (which would break the definitional link and force a classical bridge at every interconversion).

**`abbrev` for the unconditional `C = ∅` shorthand; `def` with explicit `(hJ : G.J = ∅)` for the `J = ∅` aliases.** LN item 3 is pure notation: `A ⫫ⁱˢ_G B` *means* `A ⫫ⁱˢ_G B | ∅`, no new concept. `abbrev IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty` is fully transparent — Lean reduces `G.IsISigmaSeparatedEmpty A B hA hB` to `G.IsISigmaSeparated A B ∅ hA hB (Set.empty_subset _)` at every use site without an `unfold` step, and lemmas targeting either form fire automatically on the other. The third subset hypothesis is discharged inline via `Set.empty_subset _`, keeping the call site to two arguments `(hA hB)` to match the LN notation (no `C` mentioned). LN item 4 is a *renaming under a hypothesis*: $\sigma$-separation is defined only when `G.J = ∅`. The two $\sigma$ aliases (`IsSigmaSeparated`, `IsNotSigmaSeparated`) take an explicit `(hJ : G.J = ∅)` premise and are encoded as `def`, not `abbrev`: with `hJ` as a dependent hypothesis, `abbrev`-grade reducibility would unfold the alias eagerly and erase `hJ` from goal displays at unrelated tactic steps, dissolving the $\sigma$-vs-$i\sigma$ symbolic distinction the LN takes care to maintain. The premise is the bare equation `G.J = ∅` rather than a routed `(hDMG : G.IsDMG)`, to avoid making def_3_18 transitively depend on def_3_7's `IsDMG` predicate (which it otherwise does not need); consumers preferring the named property can unfold `IsDMG` themselves.

**`Walk.IsSigmaBlockedGiven` reused verbatim from `def_3_17`.** The LN's ``$\pi$ is $\sigma$-blocked by $C$'' is exactly that predicate. Dot-notation `π.IsSigmaBlockedGiven C` reads as the LN does and is the unit the walk universal quantifies over. Encoding the blocking condition inline via the existential disjunction of `def_3_17` was rejected: it would duplicate the `SigmaBlockedWalks.lean` body and break the per-row LN-grep correspondence. Downstream proofs needing the unfolded existential can `unfold IsSigmaBlockedGiven` at the use site.

**Symmetry claim explicitly excluded from this row.** The LN's embedded `claimmark` for $\sigma$-separation symmetry is intentionally not formalised here, per the rewritten canonical tex's ``Treatment of the trailing LN remark'' paragraph and the operator addition `[sigma_symmetry_claim_invokes_unstated_reversal_invariance]`. The statement and proof live in the dedicated separate row `claim_3_22` (`SigmaSeparationSymmetric`); the LN's informal justification (``the set of walks between $A$ and $B$ is the same regardless of direction'') is to be read precisely as: walk-reversal is an involution on the set of walks in $G$, and the $\sigma$-blocking conditions on internal positions are stated invariantly under that involution — content of `claim_3_22`'s proof, not of this definition.
